name: Hillhouse Investment
shortDescription: Asia-focused alternative investor founded by Lei Zhang in 2005 with a $20M seed from the Yale endowment; one of the largest healthcare allocators in China, with stakes across BeiGene, Innovent, Junshi, and a 2020 spin-out (GL Ventures) for early-stage biotech.
narrativeHook: Lei Zhang took $20M from David Swensen's Yale endowment in 2005 and built it into one of Asia's largest investors; by 2021 Hillhouse managed about $73B and had become a serial co-lead in Chinese biotech, anchored by an eight-round run into BeiGene starting with the 2014 Series A.

notableExits.role: co-lead Series A and repeat investor (8 rounds)
early and growth-stage investor (participated through Series D)
post-IPO investor in A-share and HK lines

investorContext.hookFact: Hillhouse co-led BeiGene's 2014 Series A and went on to invest in eight separate BeiGene rounds, including a ~$1B 2020 follow-on; in 2020 it spun out GL Ventures (~RMB 10B / $1.4B) with biomedicine and devices as one of four core verticals.

name: Qiming Venture Partners
shortDescription: Early-and-growth-stage VC with a deep healthcare practice; led Zai Lab's $30M Series A in 2014 and CanSino's 2015 Series B, and was the only institutional investor (with Ignition) behind Tigermed's 2012 ChiNext IPO.
signature: Cross-border venture capital with a dedicated healthcare team
narrativeHook: Gary Rieschel and Duane Kuang launched Qiming in early 2006 with a $193M Fund I; by 2024 the firm managed roughly $9.5B across eleven USD funds and seven RMB funds, with healthcare partner Nisa Leung leading the seed-and-Series-A checks into Tigermed (2008), Zai Lab (2014) and CanSino (2015) — three of the defining names in China's first innovator generation.

notableExits.role: first institutional investor ($5M in 2008, follow-on 2010); only institutional backer alongside Ignition Partners at ChiNext IPO

investorContext.hookFact: Qiming's healthcare practice has been led since 2006 by managing partner Nisa Leung (who stepped down in February 2025); the firm reports >100 healthcare portfolio companies and disclosed $9.5B AUM across eighteen USD/RMB funds as of 2024.

name: Lilly Asia Ventures
headquarters: Shanghai (with offices in Hong Kong and Palo Alto)
shortDescription: Biotech-dedicated VC originally launched as Eli Lilly's Asia corporate-venture arm in 2008; spun out as an independent firm in 2011 raising third-party LP capital from Fund III onward.
narrativeHook: Yi Shi launched LAV inside Eli Lilly's Asia corporate-venture group in 2008, then took the firm independent in 2011 — by Fund VI's 2021 close at $1.35B and Fund VII's 2025 close at $700M, LAV had become one of the largest biotech-dedicated VC platforms in Asia, with confirmed early checks into Innovent (2011 Series A co-lead with F-Prime), CanSino (2013 Series A) and later Gracell (2019 Series B, exited to AstraZeneca for ~$1.2B in 2024).

notableExits.role: Series A co-lead with F-Prime Capital (~$5M round)
Series B and Series C investor; exited via AstraZeneca acquisition (~$1.2B, 2024)

investorContext.hookFact: LAV originated in 2008 as Eli Lilly's Asia corporate-venture arm and became operationally independent in 2011, raising from third-party LPs starting with Fund III; Fund VI closed at $1.35B in February 2021 and Fund VII closed at $700M in May 2025.

name: HongShan (formerly Sequoia China)
shortDescription: Originally Sequoia Capital China, founded 2005 by Neil Shen and Zhang Fan; the June 2023 Sequoia three-way split (US/EU → Sequoia Capital, India/SE Asia → Peak XV, China → HongShan) became operationally effective on 31 March 2024. AUM ~$56B at split; healthcare is one of three core practices.
narrativeHook: After Sequoia Capital announced the three-way separation of its global affiliates on 6 June 2023 over US-China geopolitical risk, Neil Shen took the China business independent and rebranded it HongShan ('redwood') by 31 March 2024 — managing roughly $56B AUM and making explicit what had effectively been the case for a decade: China venture capital is a fully separate ecosystem from Silicon Valley's.

notableExits.role: angel + Series A investor (Samantha Du founded Zai Lab in 2014 after leading healthcare at Sequoia China)

investorContext.hookFact: The June 6, 2023 Sequoia announcement of a three-way split (US/Europe → Sequoia Capital, India/SE Asia → Peak XV, China → HongShan), completed by 31 March 2024, was an explicit acknowledgement that cross-Pacific venture capital had become geopolitically untenable; HongShan retained roughly $56B AUM.
